narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 68% chance that you would not pass—well above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic and background factors like grades, test scores, and family circumstances, while setting aside demographics like gender or race. In your case, the model zeroed in on your class rankings from early undergraduate years as the strongest signals pointing toward a higher risk of bar failure. Specifically, your position in the bottom decile for first-year grades stood out as a major push toward this prediction, since students who passed the bar typically ranked much higher, around the sixth decile on average. Your third-year decile ranking, also in the bottom tier compared to passers' average of nearly sixth, reinforced that concern as the second-biggest influence, suggesting the model saw persistent challenges in academic standing. While your upper-middle family income bracket actually worked in your favor—aligning well with the majority of bar passers and nudging the prediction slightly toward success—it wasn't enough to offset those top academic red flags. For context, your LSAT score of 32 and undergraduate GPA of 2.90 are below the averages for those who passed (37.27 and 3.24, respectively), and being full-time aligns with most successful students, but neither shifted the balance as much as your deciles did. Overall, your profile compared to typical admitted students shows stronger alignment in enrollment status and income, but the early low rankings mirrored patterns of students who struggled on the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's the model highlighting where your record most closely resembles past failures. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters from professors who can speak to your growth since those early years. Many students in similar positions have improved their profiles and succeeded; reaching out to admissions advisors for targeted feedback could help tailor your next application. With focused effort on those academic standouts, you can shift the odds in your favor.
